revision_history: v1 (2026-05-17): full 6-stage synthesis. Critical Stage 3 source-attribution fix: the Bernama URL https://www.bernama.com/en/general/news.php?id=1814136 cited in Stage 1 as a 7 April 2026 source is in fact a 15 February 2020 article about Senator P. Waytha Moorthy on an earlier iteration of the same bill — removed from all card source lists; replaced with The Star / Malay Mail / FMT 7 April 2026 sources. Critical Stage 3 + Stage 2 over-personalisation fix: 'Anwar pledged the same in 2018 and 2022' → 'Pakatan Harapan, Anwar's coalition, pledged the same in 2018 and 2022, then withdrew in November 2023'. Critical Stage 3 statutory-strengthening: card 2 anchor for the never-used MRHA restraining-order claim upgraded from the 12 February 2007 Singapore parliamentary disclosure (with subsequent silence through 2024) to Singapore Home Affairs Minister K. Shanmugam's affirmative confirmation during the 4 February 2025 Maintenance of Racial Harmony Bill debate that the MRHA's RO powers have never been used in 30+ years. Critical Stage 3 Zimbabwe correction: '10-year mandate' qualifier expanded — the NPRC's enabling Act passed in 2018, five years after the 2013 constitutional amendment, so the commission had ~5 operational years; the synthesis adds the delayed-operationalisation cause without dropping the closure-without-Gukurahundi-outcome finding. Stage 3 + Stage 4 multi-cause correction: 'failure traced to political will, not framework design' → 'failure traced to delayed operationalisation and lack of political will'. Stage 4 added context: 2015 Sedition Act amendments added protection for Islam and criminalisation of secession calls (added to Card 1 sub — bridges the East Malaysia geographic-bias flag from Gemini). Stage 3 critical verification gap: 'The Malaysian draft does not propose to repeal the Sedition Act' is UNVERIFIED (no public draft text) — rephrased to 'The 2026 Malaysian announcement does not address Sedition Act repeal' (verified by absence in announcement + 12 May 2026 ministry clarification which enumerates what the bill WILL preserve, Art 3 / 153 / Malay Rulers, but does not mention Sedition Act). Stage 3 CIJ percentage correction: '84% rise' (which 36 vs 19 arithmetically does not equal — gives 89.5%) → 'nearly double 2024' — preserves the rise without committing to the disputed percentage. Stage 4 ADDED 12 May 2026 ministry clarification context (Cabinet/AGC/Dewan Rakyat track still pending; proposal may be discontinued) — incorporated in synthesis_notes and view card. Stage 5 anger-at-process pressure: addressed by adding the November 2014 Najib reversal (verified by East Asia Forum + multiple contemporaneous sources) — names the process-level constraint without characterising any community.
_provenance.discipline: 3R sensitivity — HIGH. Synthesis preserved the brief's discipline: critique remains directed at process (14-year non-delivery, comparator-selection signal, 2014 UMNO-assembly reversal of Najib's pledge, 2023 Anwar withdrawal of repeal pledge, 2015 statutory amendments expanding the Act's protective scope) and at named statutes. No characterisation of any religion, race, or royal institution. Card 1 sub names 'Islam' and 'Sabah and Sarawak' because those are the specific protective categories the 2015 Sedition Act amendments enumerate — descriptive of the statute, not characterisation of any community. Card 3 sub names 'priests, monks or imams' because those are the categories enumerated in Singapore MRHA s.8 — descriptive of the foreign statute. The 12 May 2026 ministry clarification confirming the SKN will not alter Articles 3 (Islam), 153 (Bumiputera), or the position of the Malay Rulers is acknowledged in synthesis_notes but not surfaced in the cards because it is downstream policy framing not editorially load-bearing in a 6-card budget.

headline: Harmony Bill back to drafting after 14 years, cites Singapore, Australia, Zimbabwe.
context: On 7 April 2026, National Unity Minister Aaron Ago Dagang said JPNIN had restarted drafting the National Harmony and Reconciliation Commission Bill, citing Singapore, Australia and Zimbabwe. Earlier drafts from 2014 were shelved by 2020.

cards.big: On 7 April 2026, Putrajaya picked Singapore, Australia and Zimbabwe as models for the new Harmony Bill: 'mediation over punishment'.
cards.sub: Three earlier NUCC bills drafted in 2014 were shelved by 2020. The named comparators have track records worth reading.
revision_tags: CORRECTED (Stage 3): added '2026' to the date for unambiguous reference

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'It is the fourth draft in 14 years' → 'Three earlier NUCC bills drafted in 2014 were shelved by 2020' (Stage 3 flagged the bare '4th draft' count as misleading — clearer to say three NUCC bills + 2026 revival, totalling four attempts, but framed by what was drafted not by an aggregate count)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'withdrawn, rejected or shelved' simplified to 'shelved' — two NUCC bills were rejected, one was agreed in principle 2019 then April 2020 Cabinet decided not to proceed; 'shelved' is the accurate compression for all three outcomes
sources: Malay Mail, 7 April 2026, 'Malaysia looks to Singapore, Australia, Zimbabwe for new harmony and reconciliation Bill, eyes mediation over punishment': https://www.malaymail.com/news/malaysia/2026/04/07/malaysia-looks-to-singapore-australia-zimbabwe-for-new-harmony-and-reconciliation-bill-eyes-mediation-over-punishment/215419

The Star, 7 April 2026, 'Putrajaya to revisit proposed national harmony bill, says Aaron': https://www.thestar.com.my/news/nation/2026/04/07/putrajaya-to-revisit-proposed-national-harmony-bill-says-aaron

FMT, 7 April 2026, 'Govt renews push for national harmony, reconciliation commission': https://www.freemalaysiatoday.com/category/nation/2026/04/07/govt-renews-push-for-national-harmony-reconciliation-commission
The Star, 8 April 2026 follow-up, 'Unity Bill to be revisited': https://www.thestar.com.my/news/nation/2026/04/08/unity-bill-to-be-revisited

The Star, 12 May 2026, 'Claims against SKN misleading': https://www.thestar.com.my/news/nation/2026/05/12/claims-against-skn-misleading — Ministry clarification confirming proposal remains in engagement, will preserve Articles 3 (Islam), 153 (Bumiputera), 32/38 (Conference of Rulers and Yang di-Pertuan Agong); aligns with the Aaron 7 April announcement framing.

REMOVED in v1 of synthesis: Bernama URL https://www.bernama.com/en/general/news.php?id=1814136 — Stage 3 verified this URL is a 15 February 2020 article about Senator P. Waytha Moorthy on an earlier iteration of the bill, NOT the Aaron Ago Dagang 7 April 2026 announcement. The Aaron 7 April 2026 statement is sufficiently sourced by Malay Mail + The Star + FMT above.

cards.big: Najib pledged in 2012 to repeal the Sedition Act. Anwar's coalition pledged the same in 2018 and 2022, then withdrew in November 2023.
cards.sub: Parliament amended the Act in 2015 to protect Islam and criminalise Sabah-Sarawak secession calls. CIJ logged 36 sedition cases in 2025, nearly double 2024.
revision_tags: CORRECTED (Stage 3 + Stage 2): 'Anwar pledged the same in 2018 and 2022' → 'Anwar's coalition pledged the same in 2018 and 2022, then withdrew in November 2023'. Stage 3's correction: 2018 and 2022 pledges were Pakatan Harapan manifesto pledges, not personal Anwar pledges, so 'Anwar's coalition' is the verified attribution. Stage 2's same critique was the OVERCLAIM flag. The November 2023 withdrawal is verified independently (Madpet, Aliran, BenarNews) and addresses the brief's central historical thread.

ADDED (Stage 4): '2015 amendment added protection for Islam and criminalised secession calls' — this is the legislative ratchet that makes repeal harder (UMNO and BN added these in 2015 after Najib's November 2014 reversal), and it bridges into the East Malaysia geographic-bias flag Gemini raised because Sabah/Sarawak MPs now have a stake in the Act's secession-criminalisation provision

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'an 84% rise from the year before' → 'nearly double 2024'. Stage 3 verified CIJ was reported as saying 36 vs 19, but arithmetic gives 89.5% not 84%, and the primary CIJ 2025 report was not located. 'Nearly double' preserves the rise without committing to the disputed percentage.
sources: Najib repeal pledge, 11 July 2012, Putrajaya — contemporaneous Bernama, Reuters, The Star, BBC coverage.

Najib reversal, November 2014, after UMNO General Assembly conservative pressure — Reuters 27 November 2014, https://www.reuters.com/article/world/malaysias-najib-backtracks-on-repealing-sedition-law-idUSKCN0JB0K3/; East Asia Forum 16 October 2014, https://eastasiaforum.org/2014/10/16/surge-of-sedition-charges-in-malaysia-arrests-najibs-reform-agenda/

Sedition Act 2015 amendment (Act A1485): added s.3(1)(ea) re sanctity of Islam; added s.3(1)(f) re secession; Sedition (Amendment) Act 2015 text: https://www.moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/HASUTAN/SEDITION%20ACT%201948%20AMENDMENT%202015.pdf — verified Stage 4 ADDITION.

Pakatan Harapan 2018 election manifesto, Promise 27, p.62: 'The Pakatan Harapan Government will revoke the following laws: Sedition Act 1948...' — Article 19 archive: https://www.article19.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Sedition-Act-Repeal.pdf

Pakatan Harapan 2022 election manifesto, free-speech section, pledged 'review and repeal provisions of acts that can be abused to restrict free speech' — IBAHRI joint letter 31 May 2023, https://www.ibanet.org/IBAHRI-joint-letter-on-Malaysia-Sedition-Act

Anwar November 2023 withdrawal of repeal pledge — Madpet press release November 2023; BenarNews 6 September 2023, https://www.benarnews.org/english/news/malaysian/dissent-concerns-09062023141252.html; Aliran; Article 19.

CIJ Malaysia 2025 annual monitoring report — reported by Daily Express 10 December 2025 republishing FMT, https://www.dailyexpress.com.my/news/272227/sedition-act-cases-up-84-in-2025-says-cij; cross-cited The Star focus piece 15 December 2025. Stage 3 noted the percentage arithmetic gives 89.5% not 84% and the primary CIJ report was not located through their search; T4A synthesis uses 'nearly double 2024' to preserve the verified direction while sidestepping the disputed percentage.
Sedition Act 1948 (Revised 1969), Act 15, AGC consolidated text — in force.
T4A issue 1980 ('Zahid wants firmer law on insults; sedition cases already up 84% in 2025'), published 15 May 2026.

cards.big: Singapore's Maintenance of Religious Harmony Act took effect in 1992. In February 2025, Minister Shanmugam confirmed its restraining-order powers had never been used.
cards.sub: The Minister can silence priests, monks or imams inciting religious hostility. Two years jail on breach. The threat does the work.
revision_tags: CORRECTED (Stage 3) + STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): 'No restraining order under it appears in the public record' → 'In February 2025, Home Affairs Minister K. Shanmugam confirmed its restraining-order powers had never been used'. Stage 3 said the original Wikipedia-via-2007-Hansard chain was acceptable but Stage 3 should chase any later disclosure. T4A synthesis found Shanmugam's affirmative 4 February 2025 confirmation during the Maintenance of Racial Harmony Bill 2025 debate (Parliament Hansard, multiple outlets) — this is the post-2007 disclosure that closes the evidentiary gap and strengthens the card to a directly sourced ministerial confirmation. Stage 5's general 'soften the hedge' pressure is also addressed: 'never been used' is the verified active phrasing.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): '1992' → '1992. In February 2025, ...' adds a specific verified anchor that supersedes the original 2007-Hansard-plus-silence chain. Primary source: Singapore Parliament debate 4 February 2025 on Maintenance of Racial Harmony Bill 2025, with Shanmugam stating the MRHA's similar powers have never been used in over 30 years.
sources: Singapore Maintenance of Religious Harmony Act 1990 (Act 26 of 1990), in force 31 March 1992 — Singapore Statutes Online: https://sso.agc.gov.sg/act/MRHA1990. Sections 8 and 9 (Minister for Home Affairs may issue restraining order against priests, monks, pastors, imams, elders, office-bearers and other persons in authority in religious groups).
MRHA Section 16: criminal penalty on breach of restraining order — up to S$10,000 fine and/or 2 years imprisonment for first offence; up to S$20,000 and/or 3 years for repeat.

Singapore Parliament debate on Maintenance of Racial Harmony Bill 2025, 4 February 2025: Home Affairs Minister K. Shanmugam stated that the similar restraining-order powers in the MRHA have never been used in over 30 years. Reported by multiple outlets including Xinhua, Business Standard, The Online Citizen, Connected to India. URL: https://english.news.cn/20250205/d4af9c92fb074a27809cd2800dbd3b1a/c.html and https://www.theonlinecitizen.com/2025/02/05/shanmugam-defends-maintenance-of-racial-harmony-bill-but-leaves-concerns-of-enforcement-unresolved/
Singapore Maintenance of Religious Harmony (Amendment) Act 25 of 2019 — added online-content takedown power and removed the 14-day notice period prior to issuance.
Singapore Maintenance of Racial Harmony Act 2025 (Act 8 of 2025), first reading 7 January 2025, passed 4 February 2025 — primary text Singapore Statutes Online; Bernama and Xinhua coverage.

Earlier 12 February 2007 Singapore Parliament written answer (Minister Wong Kan Seng) — confirming no MRHA restraining order had been issued since enactment; superseded but not contradicted by the 2025 Shanmugam confirmation.

cards.big: Zimbabwe's National Peace and Reconciliation Commission closed in 2023 with no Gukurahundi outcome. Its enabling Act took five years to pass.
cards.sub: Over 20,000 died in the 1983–87 atrocities. Independent reviews trace the failure to delayed operationalisation and lack of political will.
revision_tags: CORRECTED (Stage 3 + Stage 4 counter-argument): 'ran a 10-year mandate that ended in 2023' → 'closed in 2023 with no Gukurahundi outcome. Its enabling Act took five years to pass'. Stage 3 flagged that the constitutional 10-year clock started in 2013 but the enabling NPRC Act Chapter 10:32 only came into force on 1 January 2018 — so the commission was operational for ~5 years, not 10. Stage 4 surfaced parliamentarian Tendai Biti's quote that the commission 'lost five years.' T4A synthesis names the delayed-operationalisation explicitly without dropping the closure-without-Gukurahundi-outcome finding.

CORRECTED (Stage 3 + Stage 4): 'Independent reviews trace the failure to political will, not framework design' → 'Independent reviews trace the failure to delayed operationalisation and lack of political will'. Stage 3 said original wording 'over-narrows the causal account' (sources also cite underfunding, independence concerns, institutional constraints). T4A synthesis keeps the political-will finding (which Grok also wanted strengthened) but adds delayed-operationalisation as the second documented cause, avoiding the binary 'not framework design' that Stage 3 flagged as overclaim.
sources: Constitution of Zimbabwe Amendment (No. 20) Act 2013, Chapter 12, sections 251–253: establishment of the NPRC with a 10-year statutory term.
National Peace and Reconciliation Commission Act, Chapter 10:32 of 2018 — operationalising statute, came into force 1 January 2018 (five years after the constitutional amendment) — Veritas Zimbabwe.

NPRC Annual Report 2023: https://www.nprc.org.zw/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/NPRC-ANNUAL-REPORT-2023.pdf — covers the final year; programs centred on complaints handling, conflict prevention, healing and reconciliation; no Gukurahundi-specific reconciliation outcome recorded.

Zimbabwe Independent / Newsday, 11 September 2023, 'Peace commission's term ends in failure': https://www.newsday.co.zw/theindependent/local-news/article/200016516/peace-commissions-term-ends-in-failure

Zimbabwe Supreme Court judgment SC119-21, Minister of Justice v Concilia Chinanzvavana and the NPRC: https://www.lawportalzim.co.zw/cases/civil/3499/minister-of-justice-legal-and-parliamentary-affairs-and-others/concilia-chinanzvavana-and-the-national-peace-and-reconciliation-commission — set aside High Court order that would have deemed term to run from 5 January 2018 to 2028.
Parliamentarian Tendai Biti's 'lost five years' framing: https://www.thezimbabwean.co/2023/05/govt-to-transfer-nprc-functions-to-zhrc/ (Stage 4 ADDED).
African Security Review Vol 31 No 3, 2022, 'The Zimbabwe Peace and Reconciliation Commission': https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/10246029.2022.2086475

Kujenga Amani / SSRC, 7 February 2019, 'One Year After: Has the National Peace and Reconciliation Commission Act Failed Zimbabweans?': https://kujenga-amani.ssrc.org/2019/02/07/one-year-after-has-the-national-peace-and-reconciliation-commission-act-failed-zimbabweans/

CCJPZ and Legal Resources Foundation joint report 1997, 'Breaking the Silence, Building True Peace': 20,000+ death estimate for Gukurahundi atrocities of 1983–1987. Stage 3 noted estimates vary and recommended 'over 20,000' as lower-bound canonical.

cards.big: After four attempts in 14 years, the Harmony Bill is back in draft. The Sedition Act repeal is not.
revision_tags: CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'After four drafts, the Harmony Bill exists' → 'After four attempts in 14 years, the Harmony Bill is back in draft'. Stage 3 flagged the original as too strong because the 12 May 2026 ministry clarification says the proposal is still in engagement, has not been through Cabinet/AGC/Dewan Rakyat, and may be discontinued. T4A synthesis uses 'back in draft' (verified by the 7 April 2026 announcement of restarted drafting) and preserves the 'four attempts' count which Stage 3 said is supportable when framed as 3 NUCC bills + 2026 revival rather than as an aggregate count.
Reframe template check: parallel-structure form ('After X, Y. The Z is not.') — distinct from recent reframe shapes in T4A issues 1980, 1982, 1989, 1990. Validator drift check passes.
sources: Verified anchors: (a) four iterations of Harmony framework drafting attempts (2012 Najib announcement; 2014 NUCC three bills; 2019 'agreed in principle' on Commission Bill alone; 2026 redraft) per Aaron Ago Dagang 7 April 2026 statement and multi-outlet 7 April 2026 reporting (Malay Mail, The Star, FMT) — corrected from Stage 3's misleading-framing flag by keeping the count grounded in the four named iterations rather than the bare 'fourth draft' label; (b) 14-year span from 11 July 2012 Najib pledge to 7 April 2026 redraft announcement; (c) Sedition Act 1948 remaining in force per AGC consolidated text, with Anwar November 2023 withdrawal of repeal pledge (Madpet, Aliran, BenarNews); (d) 12 May 2026 ministry clarification confirming 2026 proposal still in engagement, will preserve Articles 3, 153, Conference of Rulers — does not mention Sedition Act repeal.

cards.big: Singapore's Act has issued no restraining order in 30 years. Zimbabwe delivered no Gukurahundi outcome. The 2026 Malaysian announcement does not address Sedition Act repeal.
revision_tags: CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Singapore's Act sits unused' → 'Singapore's Act has issued no restraining order in 30 years'. Stage 3 flagged 'sits unused' as too broad (warnings and close-call episodes are in the public record). T4A synthesis narrows to the specific verified finding (no formal restraining order issued) and adds the 30-year duration anchor that Shanmugam confirmed in February 2025.

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Zimbabwe's commission delivered nothing' → 'Zimbabwe's commission delivered no Gukurahundi outcome'. Stage 3 + Stage 4 both flagged the original as overclaim — the NPRC's 2023 annual report does record complaints handling, mediation, and community work; what it did not deliver is Gukurahundi reconciliation. T4A synthesis tightens to the specific verified absence.

CORRECTED (Stage 3 + REMOVED Stage 1's 'both turned on political will'): the 'political will' framing has been moved into Card 4 (where it is paired with delayed operationalisation per Stage 3's multi-cause correction). The view's third sentence had to do new work. T4A synthesis chose to land the verifiable Malaysian finding (the 2026 announcement does not address Sedition Act repeal) rather than re-state the comparator-lesson abstraction.

CORRECTED (Stage 3 UNVERIFIED flag): 'The Malaysian draft does not propose to repeal the Sedition Act' → 'The 2026 Malaysian announcement does not address Sedition Act repeal'. Stage 3 said no public draft text exists, so a claim about what the draft does or does not propose is unverifiable. T4A synthesis shifts the subject from 'the draft' (unverifiable) to 'the announcement' (verifiable — Aaron's 7 April 2026 statement and the 12 May 2026 ministry clarification both enumerate what the SKN will preserve and address, neither mentions Sedition Act repeal). This is a narrower but verified claim.
sources: Verified anchors: (a) Singapore's MRHA never-used restraining-order confirmation — K. Shanmugam, Singapore Parliament debate 4 February 2025 on the Maintenance of Racial Harmony Bill 2025 (verified by multiple outlets including Xinhua, Business Standard, The Online Citizen, Connected to India; cross-confirms the 12 February 2007 Wong Kan Seng written answer); (b) Zimbabwe NPRC closure without Gukurahundi outcome — NPRC Annual Report 2023, Zimbabwe Independent/Newsday 11 September 2023; (c) 2026 Malaysian announcement does not address Sedition Act repeal — Aaron Ago Dagang 7 April 2026 statement as reported by Malay Mail / The Star / FMT enumerates 'preventive, conciliatory, mediation, early intervention' as the SKN approach without mentioning Sedition Act repeal; 12 May 2026 ministry clarification (The Star) enumerates what the SKN will preserve (Articles 3, 153, 32/38) without mentioning Sedition Act repeal; Anwar's November 2023 withdrawal of the repeal pledge (Madpet, Aliran, BenarNews) confirms the political baseline this announcement was made on.

Kicker test (CLAUDE.md §7.6): last sentence 'The 2026 Malaysian announcement does not address Sedition Act repeal' — passes WhatsApp test (standalone editorial line, paste-able); subtraction test passes (the central narrowed finding lands even if the reader remembers only this sentence). Stage 3's UNVERIFIED flag on the original wording is addressed by reframing the subject from 'the draft' to 'the announcement'.

Primary statutory text: Singapore Statutes Online (MRHA 1990, MRHA Amendment 25/2019, MRHA Restraining Orders Regulations 2022, MRHA 2025 Bill 2/2025 / Act 8/2025); AustLII (Racial Discrimination Act 1975 ss.18B–F); Constitution of Zimbabwe Amendment 20/2013, NPRC Act Chapter 10:32 of 2018 (Veritas Zimbabwe); Federal Constitution of Malaysia (Art. 3, 8, 10, 11, 153); Sedition Act 1948 (Act 15) and Sedition (Amendment) Act 2015 (MOHA primary text); Penal Code Act 574; Pakatan Harapan 2018 manifesto (Article 19 archive). Court records: Zimbabwe Supreme Court SC119-21; Mamat bin Daud v Government of Malaysia [1988] 1 MLJ 119; Eatock v Bolt [2011] FCA 1103. Secondary 7 April 2026 announcement: Malay Mail, The Star, FMT (Bernama URL with ID 1814136 corrected and removed as it is a 15 February 2020 article about Waytha Moorthy). 12 May 2026 ministry clarification: The Star. CIJ Malaysia 2025 review reported via Daily Express/FMT 10 December 2025; SUARAM Annual Report 2025; Malaysian Bar (Ezri Abdul Wahab, 13 March 2026); Madpet November 2023; Aliran; Article 19; BenarNews; IBAHRI. Singapore MRHA never-used confirmation: K. Shanmugam, Singapore Parliament 4 February 2025 (Xinhua, Business Standard, Online Citizen). Zimbabwe NPRC track record: Veritas Zimbabwe NPRC Annual Reports 2018–2023, Newsday Zimbabwe, United Zimbabwe Alliance, African Security Review 2022, Kujenga Amani/SSRC 2019, CCJPZ + LRF 1997 'Breaking the Silence'. November 2014 Najib reversal: Reuters, East Asia Forum. Sedition Act 2015 amendment (MOHA primary text). DeepSeek-surfaced political-economy sources: Merdeka Center 2024 youth survey (acknowledged in synthesis_notes but not card-elaborated for length-budget reasons).

introduced_claims.claim: In February 2025, Home Affairs Minister K. Shanmugam confirmed [the MRHA's] restraining-order powers had never been used.
introduced_claims.verified_by: Singapore Parliament Hansard, 4 February 2025 debate on Maintenance of Racial Harmony Bill 2025. K. Shanmugam, in his speech defending the new RH Bill, stated that the similar restraining-order powers in the MRHA have been in place for over 30 years and have never been used. Multiple secondary outlets verify: Xinhua (https://english.news.cn/20250205/d4af9c92fb074a27809cd2800dbd3b1a/c.html), The Online Citizen (https://www.theonlinecitizen.com/2025/02/05/shanmugam-defends-maintenance-of-racial-harmony-bill-but-leaves-concerns-of-enforcement-unresolved/), Business Standard, Connected to India.
introduced_claims.why_introduced: Stage 1's Singapore card relied on the 12 February 2007 Wong Kan Seng written answer plus the absence of any contradicting subsequent record — Stage 1's pa_score_rationale explicitly noted this as the score-capping evidentiary gap and asked Stage 3 to chase post-2007 Hansard. Stage 3's response did not surface the 2025 Shanmugam confirmation. T4A synthesis identified it via independent web search during Stage 6 self-verification, closing the gap with a stronger primary anchor.

introduced_claims.claim: Parliament instead amended the Act in 2015 to add protection for Islam and to criminalise calls for the secession of Sabah or Sarawak.
introduced_claims.verified_by: Sedition (Amendment) Act 2015 (Act A1485) — Ministry of Home Affairs published text: https://www.moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/HASUTAN/SEDITION%20ACT%201948%20AMENDMENT%202015.pdf. Stage 4 raised the 2015 amendments and their effect as a legislative ratchet that makes repeal harder; T4A synthesis verified the underlying statutory provisions in MOHA's published text.
introduced_claims.why_introduced: Bridges two reviewer concerns: (a) Stage 2's geographic-bias flag (East Malaysia absent) — the secession criminalisation specifically protects against challenges to Sabah/Sarawak's incorporation into Malaysia, making the Act now politically salient for East Malaysia MPs; (b) Stage 5's process-naming pressure (why does the Act keep surviving) — the 2015 amendments are the answer at statute level: the Act now carries protections that have a stake-holder constituency.

introduced_claims.claim: Anwar's coalition pledged the same in 2018 and 2022, then withdrew in November 2023.
introduced_claims.verified_by: Pakatan Harapan 2018 manifesto Promise 27 p.62 (Article 19 archive); PH 2022 manifesto (cited by IBAHRI joint letter 31 May 2023); Anwar November 2023 withdrawal of repeal pledge (Madpet, Aliran, BenarNews September 2023).
introduced_claims.why_introduced: Stage 2 + Stage 3 both flagged 'Anwar pledged' as overclaim/personalisation. T4A synthesis attributes the 2018/2022 pledges to 'Anwar's coalition' (verified, the Pakatan Harapan manifestos) and adds the November 2023 withdrawal (verified) as the closing bookend. This transforms a flagged misattribution into a complete pledge-pledge-withdrawal sequence.

synthesis_notes:
1. OVER-INTEGRATION HANDLED: Stage 5 (Grok) said the analysis dodges the ethnic-political arithmetic and wanted stronger language about Malay conservative pressure. Stage 2 (Gemini) flagged 'political will failure' as evasive and wanted the same. But the 3R discipline in CLAUDE.md and in the brief (high-bar 3R sensitivity) prohibits characterisation of any community. T4A synthesis chose to (a) name the verifiable process-level event — Najib's November 2014 reversal after UMNO General Assembly conservative pressure (Reuters, East Asia Forum) — in the Card 1 source list; (b) add the 2015 Sedition Act statutory amendments (which themselves enumerate the protective categories) in Card 1 sub; and (c) acknowledge but not card-elaborate the broader ethnic-political analysis. This is a deliberate choice between Stage 5's courage critique and the brief's higher-priority 3R discipline; the synthesis lands closer to the brief's discipline because misnaming on 3R-adjacent topics carries higher T4A credibility cost than under-attacking.

2. OVER-INTEGRATION HANDLED: Stage 4 (DeepSeek) raised East Malaysia, B40, gender, rural Malay, Orang Asli, and stateless perspectives as missing. The 6-card budget cannot accommodate seven new perspectives. T4A synthesis chose the highest-leverage subset: East Malaysia is bridged via the Card 1 sub mention of the 2015 secession-criminalisation amendment (which directly affects Sabah/Sarawak political stakes). B40 and rural Malay are acknowledged in this synthesis_notes paragraph but not card-elaborated. Gender is the most regressive risk Stage 4 raised — the 2014 NUCC draft included a gender discrimination prohibition, and the 2026 redraft's content is not public — but cannot be claimed as a fact without verifiable evidence on the 2026 draft. The recommendation for a future T4A issue on the SKRN once a draft is public is to lead with the gender provision question.

3. STAGE 5 WEAKEST-CARD MISCALL: Grok identified card 6 as weakest, citing gender differentials. But Issue 1991's card 6 is the view card, not a gender card. This appears to be a card-numbering error in the Grok output (possibly Grok was running against a different schema mental model). The actual weakness flagged is moot, but the gender point is real and is addressed in synthesis_note 2 above.

4. STAGE 4 SOURCE WARNING: DeepSeek cited a Bernama URL https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php/news.php/general/sports/world/sports/general/news.php/?id=2389379 for the Singapore MRHA 2025 Act passage. This URL is malformed (duplicated path segments). T4A synthesis instead anchors the Singapore 2025 Act claim on Singapore Statutes Online (primary), Singapore Parliament Hansard 4 February 2025 (primary), and Xinhua / The Online Citizen / Business Standard (secondary).

5. COMPRESSION DROPS ACKNOWLEDGED: (a) Card 1 sub dropped the explicit percentage on CIJ figures (Stage 3 said the 84% is arithmetically inconsistent with 19→36); 'nearly double 2024' preserves the rise without committing to the disputed exact percentage. (b) Card 6 view dropped the 'both turned on political will' synthesis sentence to make room for the more concrete 'announcement does not address Sedition Act repeal' kicker; the political-will analysis is preserved in Card 4 (Zimbabwe) sub.

6. THE STORY HOLDS: Stage 3's most serious finding (Bernama URL error) was a source-attribution bug, not a claim-substance problem. Stage 3's second-most-serious finding (CIJ percentage arithmetic) was a precision issue softened with 'nearly double'. Stage 3's UNVERIFIED flag on 'draft does not propose repeal' was addressed by reframing the subject from 'the draft' (unverifiable) to 'the announcement' (verifiable absence). After all corrections, the central editorial finding survives: in 14 years, the Harmony Bill has been drafted four times and repeal of the Sedition Act has not been drafted at all.

_self_check.four_cardinal_sins_check: OVERCLAIM: 'never been used' in Card 2 big is the strongest possible claim, but it is the verified ministerial statement (Shanmugam, 4 February 2025) so it is the verified harshest accurate phrasing per Stage 1 preamble §UNDERCLAIM rule. UNDERCLAIM: the synthesis kept 'no Gukurahundi outcome' (verified) rather than softening to 'limited Gukurahundi progress' which Stage 3 said the record would not support. MISLEADING_FRAMING: removed the 'fourth draft exists' framing (Stage 3 flag) and the 'sits unused' / 'delivered nothing' compressions (Stage 3 + Stage 4 flags). UNVERIFIED_DETAIL: removed the Bernama URL bug; reframed 'Malaysian draft does not propose repeal' (unverified) to 'Malaysian announcement does not address repeal' (verified by absence in both 7 April and 12 May 2026 statements).
_self_check.3R_special_caution: Higher-bar 3R discipline maintained. No characterisation of any religion, race, or royal institution. Card 1 sub names 'Islam' and 'Sabah or Sarawak' descriptively because the 2015 Sedition Act amendment enumerates those as protected categories — verification is the statute's own text. Card 3 sub names 'priests, monks or imams' descriptively because Singapore MRHA s.8 enumerates those as the persons against whom restraining orders may be issued — verification is the foreign statute's own text. Card 4 names Gukurahundi — verified historical event with established casualty estimate. The 12 May 2026 ministry clarification (preserving Articles 3, 153, Conference of Rulers) is acknowledged in synthesis_notes but not card-surfaced.
_self_check.em_dash_check: Zero em-dashes (U+2014) in headline, context, or any card big/sub. En-dash (U+2013) used only in '1983–87' (Card 4 sub) — standard typography for date ranges, not the LLM-tell U+2014 character.
_self_check.scaffolding_marker_check: No sentence in any reader-facing field begins with 'However,' 'Moreover,' 'Furthermore,' 'Crucially,' 'Notably,' 'Importantly,' 'That said,' or 'Of course'.
_self_check.passive_voice_check: Card 2 big uses 'took effect' (intransitive) and 'confirmed' (active). Card 2 sub uses 'silence' (active), 'inciting' (participle, not passive). Card 4 big uses 'closed' (intransitive — Stage 1's 'ran a 10-year mandate' was a borderline construction; Stage 6 simplified to 'closed' which is active), 'took' (active). Card 4 sub: 'died' (active), 'trace' (active). View uses 'has issued' (active perfect), 'delivered' (active), 'does not address' (active). Headline and context use 'cites' (active) and 'said had restarted' (active reporting).
_self_check.hedge_check: No hedges from the language-quality preamble list (perhaps, appears to, may have, might have, some suggest) appear in any reader-facing surface. 'Nearly double' (Card 1 sub) is a quantification not a hedge.
_self_check.weak_abstraction_check: No weak abstractions from the language-quality preamble list (explores, examines, looks at, raises questions about) appear. Verbs of action and finding used throughout: pledged, withdrew, amended, criminalised, logged, confirmed, closed, trace, delivered, address.
_self_check.one_elevated_word_rule: Single elevated word landed: 'ratchet' is implied by Card 1 sub's pledge-withdraw-amend-double sequence but not explicitly used. The card-grid prose stays plain-strong; the elevated register lives in the source notes ('legislative ratchet', 'evidentiary calibration') which are not reader-facing. Per CLAUDE.md, one elevated word is the target; this synthesis chose to land its precision through structural compression (4 PMs across 14 years × 36 cases × 0 ROs × 5 operational years) rather than through vocabulary. Acceptable judgement.
_self_check.reframe_template_drift_check: Card 5 reframe uses parallel structure 'After X, Y. The Z is not.' — distinct from question-form ('The question is not X. It is Y.' — issues 1982, 1989), declarative-pair form (issue 1980), causal-restructure form (issue 1983, 1990). Validator drift check passes.
_self_check.first_clause_concreteness_check: Hook: 'On 7 April 2026, Putrajaya picked...' — date + named actor in first 5 words. Card 1: 'Najib pledged in 2012...' — named individual + year in first 4 words. Card 2: 'Singapore's Maintenance of Religious Harmony Act took effect in 1992...' — named country + named statute + year in first 7 words. Card 3: 'Zimbabwe's National Peace and Reconciliation Commission closed in 2023...' — named country + named institution + year in first 7 words. Reframe: 'After four attempts in 14 years...' — number + concrete in first 4 words. View: 'Singapore's Act has issued no restraining order in 30 years.' — named country + named statute + concrete negation + 30-year anchor in first 9 words. All pass.
_self_check.dominant_emotion_check: Hook: anger-at-process (4 attempts in 14 years, three comparators have track records). Card 1: anger-at-process (pledge-pledge-withdraw-amend pattern). Card 2: anxiety-of-precedent (30 years, no use — and yet the threat works). Card 3: anxiety-of-precedent (10-year mandate, 5 operational years, no outcome). Reframe: anger-at-process. View: anger-at-process consolidated. No sadness, no generalised concern. Berger-Milkman compliant.
